title: Life Insurance Over 50 in Canada
seo_title: Life Insurance Over 50 Canada | Best Plans for Ages 50–70 | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Life insurance over 50 in Canada. Compare term, whole life and no-medical options. Get honest advice and competitive quotes for ages 50–70. Ontario-licensed advisors.

_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — Canadians 50+ comparing options, often with specific needs (estate, final expenses, mortgage). Convert to quote request.
_meta.page_role: Demographic page targeting ages 50–70. Covers: term life still available, whole life for permanent needs, no-medical for health issues. Honest about cost.
_meta.writer_focus: Respectful, empowering tone. Readers over 50 are often surprised coverage is still affordable and available. Validate their concern ('is it too late?') immediately: 'No — you still have excellent options.' Be honest about cost increases with age but focus on what's still possible.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'life insurance over 50' 2–3 times per 500 words. Use 'life insurance for Canadians in their 50s' and 'senior life insurance' naturally.
natural_variations: coverage options for Canadians past age 50
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Patricia, 57, Ottawa — just paid off her mortgage, wants $250K to cover estate taxes and leave something to her two adult children. Got 20-year term for $185/month.
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not imply coverage is very expensive or nearly impossible after 50. It's more expensive than at 35 — but still very accessible and affordable for most healthy Canadians.

page_data.heading: Life Insurance Over 50: You Have More Options Than You Think
page_data.subheading: It's not too late. Canadians between 50 and 70 can access term life, whole life, and no-medical options with competitive rates — even with some health conditions.

_writer_notes.focus: Immediately address the 'am I too late?' concern. The answer is no. Empowering opening.
key_points: Coverage is still available and often affordable in your 50s and 60s
Multiple product types available — depends on your health and goals

page_data.heading: Why Canadians Over 50 Still Need Life Insurance

_writer_notes.focus: Needs shift after 50 but don't disappear. Estate planning, final expenses, supporting dependents, paying off debts.
key_points: Estate planning: pay probate fees, estate taxes, equalize inheritance between children
Final expenses: funeral costs in Ontario average $8,000–$15,000
Mortgage still outstanding: many Canadians at 55 still have 10–15 years on their mortgage
Supporting dependents: adult children, grandchildren, or a spouse with lower CPP
Business succession: business owners need insurance to fund buy-sell agreements
Legacy: leaving a tax-free gift to children or a charity

page_data.heading: Can I Still Get Term Life Insurance at 55 or 60?

_writer_notes.focus: Yes — and be specific about what's available. Term options narrow but are still substantial.
key_points: At 55: 10-year, 20-year terms available with full underwriting. Many policies available to age 65 or 70.
At 60: 10-year term is most common. 20-year term available if insurer age limit permits (most stop at age 80).
At 65: 10-year term and some T75 policies available. Whole life or no-medical becomes more relevant.
T75/T80: term policies to age 75 or 80 — useful for covering estate planning period
Patricia example: age 57, $250K 20-year term, non-smoker, healthy = ~$185/month
Healthy non-smokers in their 50s are well-insurable at standard rates

page_data.heading: Whole Life Insurance Over 50: Permanent Estate Coverage

_writer_notes.focus: Whole life makes more sense at 50+ than at 35. Permanent needs are more apparent. Estate planning context.
key_points: Whole life guarantees coverage for life — no renewal, no lapsing
Cash value grows over time — can be useful as a tax-sheltered savings component
Good for: covering final expenses, estate equalization between heirs, charitable giving
Cost: $250K whole life at age 55, male, non-smoker = ~$350–$450/month
Budget option: smaller $50K–$100K whole life policy for final expenses

page_data.heading: No-Medical Life Insurance for Canadians Over 55

_writer_notes.focus: For those with health conditions who may not qualify for standard coverage. Honest about trade-offs.
key_points: Simplified issue (health questions, no exam): available to age 75. Coverage up to $500K for qualifying health.
Guaranteed issue (no questions at all): ages 40–85. Coverage $10K–$50K. 2-year graded benefit period.
Best for: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, previous cancer (5+ years in remission), heart conditions
Trade-off: costs more per dollar of coverage than fully underwritten term life
For final expenses ($15K–$50K): guaranteed issue whole life is a practical solution
_writer_notes.callout_after: Link to no-medical life insurance page for full detail.

_writer_notes.focus: Sample rates for $250K 10-year and 20-year term. Show both male and female, smoker and non-smoker.
key_points: Age 50, male, non-smoker, $250K 10-year term: ~$90–$115/month
Age 50, female, non-smoker, $250K 10-year term: ~$65–$85/month
Age 55, male, non-smoker, $250K 20-year term: ~$175–$225/month
Age 55, female, non-smoker, $250K 20-year term: ~$130–$170/month
Age 60, male, non-smoker, $250K 10-year term: ~$150–$190/month
Age 60, male, smoker, $250K 10-year term: ~$300–$400/month
Note: Smoker rates are roughly 2.5–3x non-smoker rates at these ages

page_data.heading: Applying for Life Insurance Over 50: What to Expect

_writer_notes.focus: Describe the application process honestly. Some policies require a medical exam; some are no-medical. Paramedical exam details.
key_points: Most fully underwritten term life policies at 50+ require a paramedical exam (blood test, urine, vitals) — done at home or clinic
Process: apply online/with advisor → medical exam scheduled within 1–2 weeks → insurer reviews → approval in 2–4 weeks
Common exclusions: pre-existing conditions may result in exclusions or rated (higher) premiums
Alternative: simplified issue if you want to skip the exam but have manageable health history

faqs.question: Can I get life insurance at 65 in Canada?
faqs.answer: Yes. Term life (10-year or T75/T80) and whole life are both available at 65 with full underwriting. Guaranteed issue whole life is available up to age 80–85 from most Canadian insurers. Your health history determines what products are available to you.
faqs._guidance: Direct answer to a very common search query.

faqs.question: Is life insurance more expensive over 50?
faqs.answer: Yes — premiums increase with age because mortality risk rises. However, for healthy non-smokers in their early-to-mid 50s, coverage is still very affordable. A healthy 52-year-old can get $250,000 of 20-year term life for roughly $150–$200/month.
faqs._guidance: Honest but reassuring. Give a real number to anchor expectations.

faqs.question: What kind of life insurance is best at 55?
faqs.answer: It depends on your need. If you have a mortgage or dependents: 10 or 20-year term is typically the best value. If you want permanent coverage for estate planning: whole life or T80. If you have health issues and want guaranteed coverage: simplified issue or guaranteed issue whole life.
faqs._guidance: Give a useful framework — not a one-size answer.

faqs.question: Is it too late to get life insurance at 60?
faqs.answer: No. Term life is still available at 60, though primarily in 10-year terms (to age 70). Whole life is available with no expiry. No-medical options are available without a doctor visit. Don't assume it's too late — speak with an advisor to see what's accessible.
faqs._guidance: Reassuring. Most readers over 60 are worried it's too late. Address this directly.

_writer_notes.focus: Reassuring CTA. Emphasize that age-specific comparison is available and that advisors understand the needs of this demographic.
key_points: Rates personalized for your age and health profile
Compare term, whole life, and no-medical in one place
Ontario-licensed advisor reviews options with you
